content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 202-203 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TH_004_page_001.jpg, 1TH_004_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Finally then, brothers and sisters, we taught you how to live in a way that pleases God — and you are already doing this — but now we ask you and urge you in the Lord Jesus to do so even more.

**2** You know the instructions we gave you through the authority of the Lord Jesus.

**3** What God wants is for you to be set apart: that you keep yourselves from sexual immorality,

**4** that each of you learns to control your own body in a way that is holy and honorable,

**5** not driven by the fever of lust like the nations who do not know God,

**6** and that no one wrongs or exploits a fellow believer in this matter. The Lord is the one who avenges all these things, as we have already warned you and made absolutely clear.

**7** God did not call us to impurity but to a life set apart.

**8** So anyone who rejects this is not rejecting a human authority — they are rejecting God himself, the one who gives you his Holy Spirit.

**9** About love for your fellow believers — you do not need anyone to write to you about that, because God himself has taught you to love one another.

**10** And you are already doing it — toward all the brothers and sisters throughout Macedonia. But we urge you, dear friends, to do so even more.

**11** Make it your ambition to live a quiet life: mind your own affairs, and work with your own hands, just as we directed you,

**12** so that you conduct yourselves honorably in the eyes of outsiders and depend on no one.

**13** We do not want you to be uninformed, brothers and sisters, about those who have fallen asleep, so that you do not grieve like the rest of humanity, who have no hope.

**14** Since we believe that Jesus died and rose again, we also believe that God will bring with Jesus those who have fallen asleep through him.

**15** We tell you this on the Lord's own authority: we who are still alive and remain until the Lord comes will by no means go ahead of those who have fallen asleep.

**16** The Lord himself will descend from heaven with a commanding shout, with the voice of an archangel, and with the trumpet call of God — and the dead in Christ will rise first.

**17** Then we who are still alive, who remain, will be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air. And so we will be with the Lord forever.

**18** Therefore encourage one another with these words.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "how to live" (ΠΩΣ ΔΕΙ ΥΜΑΣ ΠΕΡΙΠΑΤΕΙΝ):** The Greek ΠΕΡΙΠΑΤΕΙΝ literally means "to walk." Paul consistently uses "walking" as a metaphor for daily conduct and lifestyle. "How to live" communicates this as a total pattern of existence, not merely individual steps.

**Verse 3 — "set apart" (ΑΓΙΑΣΜΟΣ):** Typically rendered "sanctification" or "holiness," which in English can feel abstract and churchy. ΑΓΙΑΣΜΟΣ means being made distinct, separated for God's purposes. "Set apart" captures both the process and the purpose without ecclesiastical jargon.

**Verse 4 — "control your own body" (ΤΟ ΕΑΥΤΟΥ ΣΚΕΥΟΣ ΚΤΑΣΘΑΙ):** Literally "to acquire/possess one's own vessel." The Sinaiticus reads ΣΚΕΥΟΣ (vessel), which has been debated for centuries — does it mean "body" or "wife"? In context, Paul is addressing the individual's self-mastery over their physical existence. I rendered it as "body" to follow the most natural reading of the passage's concern with sexual ethics and self-control.

**Verse 5 — "fever of lust" (ΕΝ ΠΑΘΕΙ ΕΠΙΘΥΜΙΑΣ):** Literally "in passion of desire." The Greek ΠΑΘΟΣ here carries the sense of being overwhelmed, ruled by appetite. "Fever of lust" conveys the consuming, uncontrolled quality Paul describes — desire that burns rather than desire that is merely felt.

**Verse 6 — "wrongs or exploits" (ΥΠΕΡΒΑΙΝΕΙΝ ΚΑΙ ΠΛΕΟΝΕΚΤΕΙΝ):** Two verbs: to overstep and to take advantage. Together they paint a picture of someone crossing boundaries and then taking what is not theirs. "Wrongs or exploits" captures both the transgression and the predatory gain.

**Verse 8 — "gives you his Holy Spirit" (ΤΟΝ ΔΙΔΟΝΤΑ ΤΟ ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΑΥΤΟΥ ΤΟ ΑΓΙΟΝ):** The Sinaiticus reads the present participle ΔΙΔΟΝΤΑ — God is presently, actively giving the Spirit. This is not a one-time past gift but an ongoing reality. The placement of ΑΓΙΟΝ after ΑΥΤΟΥ in the Sinaiticus text emphasizes the Spirit as both God's own and holy.

**Verse 9 — "God himself has taught you" (ΘΕΟΔΙΔΑΚΤΟΙ):** A remarkable compound word: ΘΕΟΔΙΔΑΚΤΟΙ, "God-taught." Paul coins or adopts a term that compresses a profound claim — their capacity for mutual love is not learned from human instruction but directly implanted by God. I expanded it to make the force clear.

**Verse 11 — "Make it your ambition to live a quiet life" (ΦΙΛΟΤΙΜΕΙΣΘΑΙ ΗΣΥΧΑΖΕΙΝ):** This is one of Paul's great paradoxes: ΦΙΛΟΤΙΜΕΙΣΘΑΙ means "to be ambitious, to strive eagerly," while ΗΣΥΧΑΖΕΙΝ means "to be quiet, to be still." The tension is deliberate — pursue quietness with passion. "Make it your ambition to live a quiet life" preserves this striking juxtaposition.

**Verse 13 — "fallen asleep" (ΚΟΙΜΩΜΕΝΩΝ):** Paul deliberately uses the metaphor of sleep for death, which sets up his argument about resurrection. I retained the metaphor rather than substituting "died" because the sleep imagery is essential to Paul's rhetorical strategy — sleep implies waking.

**Verse 14 — "through him" (ΔΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΙΗΣΟΥ):** The Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΥΣ ΚΟΙΜΗΘΕΝΤΑΣ ΔΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΙΗΣΟΥ — "those having fallen asleep through Jesus." The phrase ΔΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΙΗΣΟΥ can modify either the sleeping (those who died in connection with Jesus) or the bringing (God will bring them through Jesus). The ambiguity may be intentional. I attached it to the sleeping, reading it as a description of Christian death — falling asleep "through" or "in connection with" Jesus.

**Verse 15 — "on the Lord's own authority" (ΕΝ ΛΟΓΩΙ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ):** Literally "in a word of the Lord." Paul claims direct dominical authority for what follows. Rather than the flat "by the word of the Lord," I rendered this as "on the Lord's own authority" to convey that Paul is invoking a specific teaching from the Lord, not merely offering his own opinion.

**Verse 16 — "commanding shout" (ΚΕΛΕΥΣΜΑΤΙ):** ΚΕΛΕΥΣΜΑ is a military or royal command — the shout a general gives to advance, the cry a captain gives to rowers. This is not a gentle call but a sovereign order that sets events in motion. "Commanding shout" preserves the authority and volume of the image.

**Codex Sinaiticus Observations:** The text of chapter 4 begins in the fourth column of PDF page 202 and continues into the first column of page 203. The transition from chapter 3 to chapter 4 is unmarked in the manuscript — the scriptio continua flows without break. In verse 1, the Sinaiticus includes ΚΑΘΩΣ ΚΑΙ ΠΕΡΙΠΑΤΕΙΤΕ ("as you are also walking"), confirming the commendation that they are already living this way. In verse 8, the manuscript reads ΤΟΝ ΔΙΔΟΝΤΑ (present tense "the one giving") rather than the aorist found in some later manuscripts. The eschatological section beginning at verse 13 shows no corrector marks, suggesting the scribe's original text was accepted.

— PNEUMA
